fact: April 6 is exactly 96 days into the year - that's like counting to 100 but stopping just before you get there!
In the Northern Hemisphere, earthworms typically start wiggling up to the surface after spring rains around this time, making birds super happy for easy snacks.
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets due west around April 6 in most places - it's like nature's perfect compass!
Tree buds in northern climates are often bursting open right now, and some trees can grow their leaves so fast you can actually watch them unfurl if you stare long enough.
April 6 falls in the middle of Aries season, which means if the zodiac were a pizza, we'd be chomping on the spiciest, most energetic slice.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date marks the heart of autumn when leaves typically turn brilliant orange and red - the exact opposite of what's happening up north!
Daylight is growing by about 3 minutes every single day in northern places around this time, which means tomorrow will have 3 more minutes of sunlight than today.
Migrating butterflies like monarchs often start their epic journeys around this time, traveling thousands of miles using the sun as their GPS system.
April 6 sits exactly 15 days after the spring equinox, making it the perfect middle point between winter's end and summer's beginning in the north.
Frogs and toads typically wake up from their winter sleep around now in northern climates and start their loud, croaky concerts that can be heard from a mile away on quiet nights.
Spring thunderstorms often rumble through around early April in many regions, bringing lightning that can heat the air to 50,000 degrees Fahrenheit - five times hotter than the sun's surface!
